items.title: Zero-centered hidden activations
items.scenario: A hidden layer receives pre-activations z = [−2.0, −0.5, 0.0, 0.5, 2.0]. Applying tanh gives a ≈ [−0.96, −0.46, 0.0, 0.46, 0.96] — symmetric around zero. The same z through sigmoid would yield values all in (0, 1) with mean ≈ 0.5, not centered at zero.
items.explanation: Illustrates the canonical zero-centered property: negative pre-activations produce negative activations, positive produce positive, and zero stays zero.

items.title: Classic RNN hidden update
items.scenario: At time step t, an RNN computes h_t = tanh(W_h h_{t−1} + W_x x_t + b). If the linear combination is large positive, h_t saturates near +1; large negative near −1. This keeps the hidden state bounded across long sequences.
items.explanation: Tanh is the standard activation for vanilla RNN hidden states — see Recurrent Cells for the full cell mechanics.

items.title: LSTM candidate cell with tanh
items.scenario: An LSTM computes a candidate cell ĉ_t = tanh(W_c · [h_{t−1}, x_t] + b_c) while forget and input gates use sigmoid outputs in [0, 1]. The final cell blends gated information with a tanh-bounded candidate.
items.explanation: Recurrent architectures often pair sigmoid gates (0–1 blending) with tanh on the cell candidate (±1 bounded state) — detailed in LSTM Gates.

items.title: Autoencoder output matching normalized images
items.scenario: Pixel data is scaled to [−1, 1] for training. The decoder's final layer uses tanh so reconstructions naturally lie in the same range as inputs, unlike sigmoid which would cap at (0, 1).
items.explanation: When target data is zero-centered and bounded to ±1, tanh is a natural output activation choice.
